behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The core failure here is not just whether filenames sort correctly (that is Unit) but whether the runner actually feeds the sorted list to the executor in the right order and the DB receives statements in that sequence. A pure unit test on the sort function misses the handoff from sorting to execution. REAL-INFRA: An in-process fake DB that records the order SQL statements are received is sufficient to catch this failure — no real OS, network, or subprocess is required. An in-process fake DB can record call order and verify ascending sequence. REJECTION-GATE: System is too high because no subprocess, real network, or OS filesystem event is involved — the migration directory is read in-process.
behaviors.test_description: Seed a migrations/ directory with files named 010_c.sql, 002_b.sql, 001_a.sql. Wire the runner to an in-process fake DB client that records each SQL statement received in a list. Run the runner. Assert the recorded execution list is ordered 001 → 002 → 010 by their filename prefix integers, not lexicographically.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: This behavior requires the runner to read state from the `_migrations` table on startup, compare it against discovered files, and skip matching entries. The failure mode is squarely at the module boundary between the runner's file-discovery logic and the DB state reader — a unit test of either in isolation cannot catch this. REAL-INFRA: An in-process fake DB that pre-populates `_migrations` rows is sufficient; real PostgreSQL is not required because the logic is 'query a table, compare results, skip matches' which is reproducible with a stub. REJECTION-GATE: System is too high — no subprocess or real network is needed.
behaviors.test_description: Pre-populate the fake `_migrations` table with a record for 001_a.sql. Place both 001_a.sql and 002_b.sql in the migrations/ directory. Run the runner with the in-process fake DB. Assert that only the SQL from 002_b.sql was sent to the fake DB executor, and 001_a.sql's SQL was never executed.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure has two parts: (a) transaction rollback actually undoes DB writes, and (b) the runner halts subsequent migrations. Part (b) is catchable with a mock. Part (a) requires verifying real DB state after rollback — asserting that `rollback()` was called on a mock does not prove the DB is unchanged. REAL-INFRA: This needs a real local PostgreSQL instance (a test container or local pg). The key failure — 'partial writes survive despite rollback' — is invisible to an in-process fake because the fake does not enforce ACID semantics. A real local DB is needed to verify the table is empty after the failed migration. REJECTION-GATE: System is too high — 'local DB' fits the Integration definition; no external network or real OS subprocess is required to run a local pg test container.
behaviors.test_description: Using a real local PostgreSQL (e.g., test container), place two migrations: 001_create.sql (INSERT a row into a scratch table) and 002_fail.sql (valid INSERT followed by a deliberate syntax error). Run the runner. Assert: the scratch table contains no rows from 001 (rolled back), and no record for 001 or 002 in `_migrations`. Also assert the runner did not attempt 002 after 001 failed (verify via runner return status or emitted events).

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: This is an inter-process concurrency/locking behavior. The failure mode — two processes both deciding a migration is unapplied, then both applying it — cannot appear within a single process. Any in-process simulation of two runners eliminates the real failure boundary (OS process scheduling, DB advisory lock contention across separate connection pools). REAL-INFRA: Requires spawning two real subprocesses and a real local PostgreSQL to exercise the actual lock acquisition / release path. A fake DB running in the same process shares memory and cannot reproduce concurrent lock races between separate processes. REJECTION-GATE: Integration is too low — the failure only manifests across real OS process boundaries and real DB connection isolation, which is exactly the System definition ('subprocess, OS behavior').
behaviors.test_description: Using a real local PostgreSQL and two child processes (Node.js spawn or equivalent), start both runners simultaneously against the same DB with a shared migrations/ directory containing a set of SQL files. Wait for both to exit. Assert: each migration file appears exactly once in `_migrations`, and the DB reflects each migration's effect exactly once (no duplicate rows, no missing rows).

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the `_migrations` table is created before any migration file is applied.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is an initialization-ordering bug: the runner tries to INSERT into `_migrations` after applying a migration but before ensuring the table exists, causing a runtime error on a truly fresh DB. A unit test of the bootstrap function in isolation cannot catch this because the failure only appears when the bootstrap, migration executor, and recorder modules are wired together in sequence. REAL-INFRA: A real local PostgreSQL is the most reliable approach (verifying `_migrations` actually exists before the first migration SQL is sent), but an in-process fake DB that throws on INSERT to a non-existent table also reproduces the failure. Either way, no subprocess or real network is needed — this fits the Integration definition. REJECTION-GATE: System is too high because the failure boundary is between local modules, not at OS/process/network boundaries.
behaviors.test_description: Point the runner at a real local PostgreSQL with a completely empty database (no tables). Run the runner with one migration file. Assert: (1) `_migrations` table exists in the DB after the run, (2) the migration file's SQL was executed successfully (verifiable via the table it creates/modifies), and (3) `_migrations` has exactly one record. To catch ordering bugs specifically, instrument the runner or use pg event triggers to confirm `_migrations` was created before the first migration SQL was sent.
